title: How One Mom with Brain Injury Built a 30K-Member Movement to Fight for Kids
guestBio: Amy Bohn is the President and Co-Founder of PERK (Protection for the Educational Rights of Kids), one of California's most powerful advocates for parental rights and medical freedom. After overcoming a severe brain injury in 2018, she built a movement of over 30,000 members that has driven more than 5 million advocacy actions to legislators.
excerpt: After a devastating brain injury left her unable to read or walk across a room, Amy Bohn discovered 50,000 California children were being denied education due to medical exemption laws. What she did next will inspire every parent who's ever felt powerless against the system.
metaDescription: Amy Bohn overcame brain injury to build a 30K-member movement fighting for 50K kids denied school access. Her story will inspire every parent.
keyTakeaways: Personal challenges can become the catalyst for creating massive social change when you refuse to stay silent about injustice.
Organized parent advocacy can defeat extreme legislation even in hostile political environments, as proven when PERK defeated 8 out of 12 bills in 2022.
California's medical exemption laws currently prevent 50,000 children from attending school, daycare, or college.
Legislative strategies used in California are being replicated across other states, making this a national issue requiring immediate attention.
The removal of personal belief exemptions, religious freedom, and medical autonomy happened through a deliberate brick-by-brick legislative strategy.
Doctors are being retroactively punished for providing legal medical exemptions they wrote before law changes took effect.
Building a grassroots movement requires showing up consistently, even when you feel unprepared or overwhelmed by the magnitude of the challenge.

pullQuotes.text: What started as one exhausted mom showing up at the Capitol became a movement of over 30,000 members driving millions of advocacy actions.

timestamps.label: Amy's brain injury and recovery journey
The legislative strategy behind SB 277 and SB 276
Building PERK from the ground up

content:
Sometimes the most powerful movements are born from the most unlikely circumstances. When Amy Bohn suffered a severe brain injury in 2018, she couldn't read a page out loud or walk across a room. But when she discovered that California was systematically denying 50,000 children access to education through medical exemption laws, her maternal instincts kicked into overdrive. What happened next proves that you don't have to be perfectly prepared to create massive change—you just have to be willing to show up.

In this eye-opening episode of The Manage Her, Amy takes us inside her journey from brain injury survivor to leading one of California's most influential parental rights organizations. Her story isn't just about overcoming personal adversity; it's about recognizing when something is fundamentally wrong in our society and refusing to let your own limitations become excuses for inaction.

When Personal Struggle Meets Public Injustice

Amy's transformation from recovering brain injury patient to fierce advocate wasn't planned—it was ignited by a mother's fury at discovering systematic injustice. While she was still struggling with basic cognitive functions, she learned about California's legislative assault on parental rights through bills like SB 277 and SB 276. These weren't just policy changes; they were deliberate strategies that removed personal belief exemptions, religious freedom, and medical autonomy from families across the state.

The numbers are staggering and heartbreaking: 50,000 children in California right now cannot attend school, daycare, or college because of these laws. Think about that for a moment. Fifty thousand kids denied their fundamental right to education because bureaucrats decided they knew better than parents and doctors about individual medical decisions.

What makes Amy's story so compelling is that she didn't wait until she felt ready or qualified to act. She showed up to the Capitol as one exhausted mom who could barely function, but she showed up anyway. This is the essence of real leadership—recognizing that perfect preparation is the enemy of necessary action.

Building a Movement from Ground Zero

Under Amy's leadership, PERK (Protection for the Educational Rights of Kids) has grown from that one struggling mom into a powerhouse organization with over 30,000 members. They've generated more than 5 million emails, phone calls, and measurable advocacy actions directed at legislators. But here's what's most impressive: in 2022, her coalition defeated 8 out of 12 extreme bills targeting medical freedom, proving that organized parents can win even in the most hostile political environments.

This victory didn't happen by accident. Amy and her team developed sophisticated strategies for tracking legislation, mobilizing members, and applying pressure at exactly the right moments. They learned how to read the political landscape, identify key decision-makers, and deploy their resources for maximum impact. Most importantly, they proved that grassroots organizing can overcome well-funded special interests when parents are properly motivated and organized.

The genius of Amy's approach is that she didn't try to do everything alone. She built systems and recruited other passionate parents who could contribute their unique skills to the movement. This collaborative approach allowed PERK to scale beyond what any single person could accomplish, no matter how dedicated.

The Brick-by-Brick Strategy

One of the most chilling aspects of Amy's story is how she exposes the deliberate, methodical strategy behind California's medical freedom restrictions. This wasn't random policy-making; it was a carefully orchestrated campaign that removed parental rights piece by piece, making each step seem reasonable while building toward total control.

The strategy worked by creating legislation that appeared moderate but contained provisions for future expansion of government power. Once the initial bills passed, subsequent legislation could reference the existing framework to justify even more extreme measures. Amy breaks down exactly how this brick-by-brick approach dismantled decades of established parental rights in just a few years.

Perhaps most outrageous is how doctors are being retroactively punished for providing medical exemptions that were completely legal when they wrote them. Imagine being penalized today for actions that were not only legal but medically appropriate when you took them. This retroactive enforcement creates a chilling effect where physicians become afraid to advocate for their patients' individual medical needs.

A Warning for Every State

If you think California's medical freedom restrictions are just a West Coast problem, Amy has news for you: the same playbook is being implemented across the country. The legislative strategies, the political messaging, even the specific language in bills—it's all being replicated in state after state. What happened in California is coming to your state, and the question is whether you'll be ready to fight it.

Amy's message isn't meant to create fear; it's meant to create preparedness. She's providing a roadmap for recognizing the warning signs and building the organizational capacity to respond effectively. Her experience proves that parents can win these battles, but only if they're organized, informed, and willing to engage in the political process.

The most powerful aspect of Amy's story is how it demonstrates that your current circumstances don't determine your potential impact. She went from being unable to read a page to leading a movement that influences state legislation. She transformed personal struggle into public service and individual adversity into collective action.

The Call to Action

Amy's journey reminds us that leadership often emerges from the most unexpected places during the most challenging times. You don't have to wait until you feel qualified or prepared to stand up for what's right. You don't need permission from anyone to become an advocate for children and families.

What you need is the willingness to show up, the courage to learn as you go, and the determination to keep fighting even when the odds seem overwhelming. Amy Bohn proves that one mom with a brain injury can build a movement powerful enough to change state legislation. Imagine what you could accomplish if you stopped making excuses and started taking action.

The 50,000 children currently denied education in California are counting on adults who care more about doing what's right than doing what's comfortable. Amy answered that call despite her personal limitations. The question is: will you answer it despite yours?
